coredata.dc:title: The regulation of the microstructure, luminescence and lubricity of multi-element doped carbon nanodots with alkylated diquaternary 1, 4-Diazabicyclo[2.2.2]octane derived dicationic ionic liquids inserted in carbon skeleton
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Lina
Cai, Tao
Ye, Mengting
Liu, Dan
Liu, Shenggao
coredata.dc:description: Herein, carbon dots-ionic liquid (CDs-IL) hybrid nanoparticles are synthesized, on the one side, for constructing novel nanostructure with IL inserted into the carbon skeleton, on the other side, for integrating both excellent friction- and wear-reducing abilities. To realize the assumption, the 1,4-Diazabicyclo[2.2.2]octane was used as bridging agent to synthesize bis-functional dicationic ionic liquid with symmetric structure, followed by co-carbonization with carbon precursor to prepare target products under mild conditions. Three anions included bis(salicylato)borate (BScB), hexafluorophosphate (PF6) and bis(trifluoromethanesulphonyl)imide (NTf2) are utilized to tune their physicochemical properties. The chemical and microstructural properties of the products were characterized by NMR, FTIR, Raman mapping spectra, XPS, UV–visible spectra, Fluorescence spectra, SEM, HRTEM and so on. The produced CDs-IL with an average diameter range of 1–3 nm exhibit excitation-dependent maximum fluorescence at 340/405 nm, 440/518 nm and 380/458 nm for CDs-BScB, CDs-PF6 and CDs-NTf2, respectively. The CDs-PF6 is found to exhibit excellent anti-wear and friction-reducing properties as nanoadditives for PEG base fluid, which achieves remarkably improved anti-wear properties (96.5%) and friction-reducing (72.7%) properties simultaneously, owing to the formation of a protective carbon-based layer at the contact regions. Importantly, friction-induced luminescence quenching, transfer and aggregation of the CDs-IL were observed and their correlations were discussed.

head.ce:title: The regulation of the microstructure, luminescence and lubricity of multi-element doped carbon nanodots with alkylated diquaternary 1, 4-Diazabicyclo[2.2.2]octane derived dicationic ionic liquids inserted in carbon skeleton

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. No. 1219, Zhongguan West Road, Ningbo City, Zhejiang Province, 315201, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, carbon dots-ionic liquid (CDs-IL) hybrid nanoparticles are synthesized, on the one side, for constructing novel nanostructure with IL inserted into the carbon skeleton, on the other side, for integrating both excellent friction- and wear-reducing abilities. To realize the assumption, the 1,4-Diazabicyclo[2.2.2]octane was used as bridging agent to synthesize bis-functional dicationic ionic liquid with symmetric structure, followed by co-carbonization with carbon precursor to prepare target products under mild conditions. Three anions included bis(salicylato)borate (BScB), hexafluorophosphate (PF6) and bis(trifluoromethanesulphonyl)imide (NTf2) are utilized to tune their physicochemical properties. The chemical and microstructural properties of the products were characterized by NMR, FTIR, Raman mapping spectra, XPS, UV–visible spectra, Fluorescence spectra, SEM, HRTEM and so on. The produced CDs-IL with an average diameter range of 1–3 nm exhibit excitation-dependent maximum fluorescence at 340/405 nm, 440/518 nm and 380/458 nm for CDs-BScB, CDs-PF6 and CDs-NTf2, respectively. The CDs-PF6 is found to exhibit excellent anti-wear and friction-reducing properties as nanoadditives for PEG base fluid, which achieves remarkably improved anti-wear properties (96.5%) and friction-reducing (72.7%) properties simultaneously, owing to the formation of a protective carbon-based layer at the contact regions. Importantly, friction-induced luminescence quenching, transfer and aggregation of the CDs-IL were observed and their correlations were discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of modern industrial technology, the increasing consumption of mechanical wear and economic losses caused by undesirable mechanical wear has attracted great attention [1–3]. In order to extend the service life of the equipment and reduce economic losses, people have been working on the development of new lubricants and lubricant additives [4–7]. Advanced lubricants have been widely used in enormous quantities of mechanical systems owing to their excellent lubricity, equipment cooling, cleaning performance and other advantages. Nevertheless, the common petroleum-based oil lubricants suffer from severe environmental problems owing to itself non-degradable property and the usage of harmful compounds as additives [8]. Recently, polyethylene glycol-based (PEG) fluid has been explored as lubricants for various situations owing to itself environmentally-friendly and degradable natures [9]. However, neat PEG shows poor lubricity in terms of friction and wear, it is still lack suitable additives to improve the lubricity and load capacity of hydrophilic PEG base fluids, therefore, nanofluids start to attract the attention of researchers.

Nanofluids are dispersions of nanoparticles in various base fluids, in which the nanoparticles are common metals, metal oxides, ceramics, carbon and its allotropes with a size in nano-scale [10,11] Nanofluids have been a hot topic of great interest in fields of chemical industry, heat transfer, and tribology [12–14]. Studies have shown that the dispersions of nano-scale particles into base fluids play a vital role in boosting the tribological and cooling properties. Among them, carbon-based materials have been most studied owing to their low-cost, non-toxic and unique physical and chemical properties [15]. In the past few decades, zero, one- and two-dimensional carbon-based solid materials and their composites, including fullerene, carbon nanotubes, and graphene have been studied and applied in the friction field to meet the requirements of high-performance nanoparticle additives [16–18]. In consideration of the use of a huge number of mechanical systems and the challenge for the energy conservation arouse a sustainable development for improving the energy efficiency and durability of lubricants, which drive the research forward in exploiting high performance nanoadditives for hydrophilic base fluids.

Till now, however, how to obtain a stable nanoparticle dispersion is still of great challenge. As a new member of carbon-based nanoparticles, carbon dots (CDs) have also received high attention in the field of tribology since 2015 [19], which have great application potential and good application prospects as nanoadditives to improve interactions between CDs and hydrophilic base fluids and tribological properties [20]. Comparing with typical carbon-based materials reported, rich in oxygen-containing groups and facile preparing methods, which are both outstanding strengths for CDs [21–25]. Nevertheless, there have been only a few reports in relation to CDs-based nanoadditives, and their synthetic route, wear-resistance performance, and functional duration of the tribofilm should be improved [26]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a facile way to synthesize CDs with novel microstructure, components and tunable properties.

It is widely accepted that the well-dispersed nanoparticles having strong interactions with metal surface are essential for achieving the full potential and distinguished performance of nanoparticles. For achieving those requirements, very recently, many efforts have been done to introduce various ionic liquids (ILs) for tuning the structure of nanoparticles, which endow them unique physicochemical properties including excellent solubility, high thermal stability and tribological performance and so on [27–36]. For instance, Guo et al. investigated the tribological behavior of ionic SiO2 nanofluids, the result showed that the nanofluids dramatically reduced the friction and wear [37]. Wang et al. modified the surface of carbon dots with ionic liquids to achieve favorable solubility in base oil [38]. Rizzo et al. reported the preparation of NCND ionogels, using dicationic organic salts as gelators, which showed a pronounced antioxidant activity [39]. Li et al. produced ionic liquid-functionalized carbon nanodots having favorable water solubility [40]. Castro et al. produced carbon nanodots (C-dots) with an average size of 1.5 and 3.0 nm by laser ablation in different imidazolium ionic liquids (ILs), where the fluorescence properties of C-dots could be influenced by the nature of the IL cation and anion [41]. Tang et al. synthesized ionic liquid capped carbon dots for water-based lubrication [42]. In those works, ILs based on the cationic moieties of imidazole are most studied, in which ILs are used in a great amount and distributed on the surface of the composites. Thus, how to synthesize carbon dots-based composites owning novel IL moiety in the carbon skeleton is challenging and yet to be explored. Moreover, the relationship between anionic moiety and friction behavior, as well as their distribution on the contact surfaces are yet to be reported.

Based on polymer chemistry, once bis-functional compounds and multi-functional compounds with reactive groups are mixed together uniformly, a condensation, cross linking and in situ carbonization reactions occurred sequentially upon heating, thus a product owning IL moiety inserted in the carbon core could be obtained in this way. The IL should have symmetric structure to ensure same reactivity of the functional group. However, it is lack suitable ionic liquids till now, which drives us to design novel ionic liquids with target structure. On the one side, cationic moieties in the carbon core may facilitate the formation of a double electric layer by electrostatic interaction, on the other hand, the carbon core can be readily adsorbed onto the rubbing surfaces due to the strong interactions between the anionic moieties in carbon core and positive charge of the tiny sites on the surface, which may act as “ball effect” and “nanometer self-healing”. To demonstrate this concept, herein, a series of bis-functional dicationic ionic liquids based on the 1,4-diazabicyclo[2.2.2]octane (DABCO-based) have been designed and synthesized for the introduce of bis-functional reactive groups, which is beneficial for the following synthesis of carbon dots-based composites. Simultaneously, owing to their unique structure and characters, their properties can be adjusted based on the altering of the structure and type of anions, which is also beneficial for tuning of thus obtaining carbon dots-based hybrid nanomaterials [43–47].

The main purpose of this work is to prepare carbon dots-based nanomaterials (CDs-IL) owning IL moieties inserted in carbon skeleton by polycondensation and carbonization between a bis-functional dicationic ionic liquid and multi-functional citric acid (Scheme 1 ). The one-pot low temperature solid-phase synthesis process we use is efficient, low cost and convenient, which is beneficial for obtaining uniform-distributed carbon dots. More importantly, the microstructures, components, photoluminescence properties of the CDs-IL can be tailored by change of ILs, their physicochemical and tribological properties were carefully studied. Impressively, a relationship between the unique fluorescence characteristics and the excellent tribological properties of carbon dots-based composites were discussed.

Citric acid monohydrate (Aladdin, 99.5%), 1,4-Diazabicyclo[2.2.2]octane (Energy Chemical, 99%), 3-Chloro-1-propanol (Aladdin, 98%), lithium carbonate (Energy Chemical, 99%), salicylic acid (Aladdin, 99.5%), boric acid (Sinopharm, AR), Potassium hexafluorophosphate ([K][PF6]) (Sigma Aldrich, 98%), Lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulphonyl)imide ([Li][NTf2]) (Aladdin, 99%), Methanol (Sinopharm, AR), Ethyl acetate (Sinopharm, AR) were used without further purification. Polyethylene glycol (PEG) with an average molecular weight of 190–210 g mol−1 (Aladdin, AR) was used as the hydrophilic synthetic base oil.

3-Chloro-1-propanol (23.635 g, 250 mmol) and 1,4-Diazabicyclo[2.2.2]octane (5.609 g, 50 mmol) were mixed with 15 ml methanol in a 250 ml three-necked flask and then the oil bath was heated to 80 °C. The reaction continued for 84 h under an argon atmosphere. The reaction mixture was recrystallized from 250 ml ethyl acetate. A white precipitate was filtered, washed and dried under vacuum (60 °C) for 12 h. Then [C3BHDABCOC3][Cl]2 was obtained.

Then [C3BHDABCOC3][Cl]2 (3.013 g, 10 mmol) was added to [K][PF6] (3.865 g, 21 mmol) aqueous solution in a 1: 2.1 M ratio at room temperature and stirred for 4 h 100 ml ethyl acetate was added to the reaction mixture to extract the target product. The ethyl acetate layer was evaporated to collect a white solid which was dried under vacuum (60 °C) for 24 h. Likewise, [C3BHDABCOC3][BScB]2 and [C3BHDABCOC3] [NTf2]2 were synthesized in a similar manner by using [Li][BScB] and [Li][NTf2], respectively, instead of [K][PF6]. Lithium carbonate (1.478 g, 20 mmol), boric acid (2.473 g, 40 mmol) and salicylic acid (11.050 g, 80 mmol) were mixed at a molar ratio of 1: 2: 4 and heated at 60 °C for 4 h. The reaction product lithium borate salt ([Li][BScB]) was used to the subsequent step with isolation. The structures and synthesis route of DABCO-based dicationic liquids are depicted in Scheme 1.

Multinuclear (1H, 13C, 11B, 31P and 19F) magnetic resonance (Fig. S1-8) and mass spectrometry were employed to further analyze the structures of dicationic ionic liquids (Fig. S9, Fig. S10). The detailed results are shown below.

In this work, three CDs-IL were prepared in a facile one-pot synthesis (Scheme 1), which is conducted by the condensation and carbonization between the DABCO-based bis-functional ionic liquid and citric acid. The molar ratio of citric acid and DABCO-based dicationic ionic liquids has significant effects on the morphology and properties of final obtained CDs-IL. After the exploration of synthetic processes, 4/1 is considered as an optimal molar ratio. Citric acid (5.2 mmol) and DABCO-based dicationic ionic (1.3 mmol) were added to 20 ml deionized water. Then the mixtures were premixed at 60 °C for 4 h. The premixed mixtures were rotary evaporated to remove most of the solvent, which were pretreated under air and then placed in a hydrothermal reactor. The brown viscous products were obtained after heating at 190 °C for 9 h and cooled them to room temperature. After dissolving the products in 20 ml water, sediments were removed by centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 10 min). The supernatants were dialyzed against 30 ml deionized water and ethanol for 12 h with molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 1000. The solvents were removed by rotary evaporator, and the residues were dried at 60 °C for 48 h.

The nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were collected on AVANCE III 400 MHz spectrometer (Bruker, USA). The positive and negative ion electrospray mass spectra were recorded using a LC-Q-TOF-MS instrument (AB Sciex, USA). The Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo-Fisher, USA) was used to record Fourier transformed infrared spectra (FTIR). Thermal stability analysis was performed on a thermogravimetric analyzer in a N2 atmosphere at a temperature in the range of 50–550 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C/min (METTLER TOLEDO, Switzerland). UV–vis spectra were obtained at room temperature using a Lambda 950 UV–visible spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, America) in the measurement range of 240–800 nm. The transmission electron microscopy images were taken with a Tecnai F20 (FEI) electron microscopy. X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) was recorded on an AXIS ULTRA (Kratos) spectrometer. The test used Al Kα as the source of radiation with an energy of 50 eV. Calibration was performed using the electronic binding energy of C1s of 284.6 eV as an internal standard. Fluorescence spectra were carried out by a Fluorescence spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Lumina) at room temperature. The excitation light used in the test has a wavelength range of 320–560 nm. The effects of CDs-IL on the viscosity properties of PEG were evaluated on kinematic viscosity tester (SHENKAI, China). The kinematic viscosity of products was determined by kinematic viscosity measurement and dynamic viscosity calculation at a temperature in the range of 40–100 °C.

The tribological properties of the CDs-IL were characterized using a four-ball tester (Tenkey, Xiamen, China): three clean 12.7 mm diameter GCr15 steel balls were clamped together in a oil cup and covered with the lubricant (test oil sample) to be evaluated. The test oil sample in the oil cup at least 3 mm above the top of the balls. A fourth 12.7 mm diameter GCr15 steel ball was used as the upper ball for three-point of contact with the three clamping balls. The test was performed by continuously rotating GCr15 steel balls at a constant speed under a constant test load and rubbing against the three clamping balls (load: 392 N; rotation rate: 1200 rpm; time: 1 h; temperature: 75 °C) (Scheme 2 ).

After the test, optical microscopy was used to measure the wear scar diameter (WSD) on the three lower clamped balls. Lubricants were compared by using the average size of the scar diameters worn on the three lower clamped balls. The curve of the coefficient of friction over time was automatically recorded by the instrument. And the diameter of the worn spot was measured by optical microscope.

Zeiss EVO18 scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Zeiss, Germany) was employed determining the surface features of the wear scar. To visualize the appearance of wear scar more intuitively, we collected 3D wear scar images on LSM 700 optical microscope (Zeiss, Germany).

Raman spectra were characterized on a Renishaw In via Reflex spectrometer (Renishaw, England). The surface of the wear spot was tested using an Ar laser source with a wavelength of 532.2 nm. The wavelength range of the test was 400–4000 cm−1. In order to find out the nature of the tribochemical film deposited under the friction stress, the element compositions and distributions of the elements on the worn surfaces were examined by Zeiss X-MAX EDX spectrometer and X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS).

Fig. 1 ., Fig. S11 and Fig. S12 show the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of CDs-IL with relatively uniform spherical shape without any obvious aggregation. For example, the CDs-PF6 was spherical particles with a narrow size distribution ranging from 1.0 to 3.2 nm. And the HRTEM image provided the distinct lattice planes with the interplanar spacing of 0.23 nm, which can be assigned to (100) facets of graphite carbon. In addition, CDs-BScB and CDs-NTf2 were also spherical particles with mean diameters of 1.1 nm, 2.9 nm respectively. And the lattice fringes with interplanar spacing of 0.23 nm were noticed in the HRTEM images of CDs-BScB and CDs-NTf2, corresponding to (100) facets of graphite carbon [48,49].

Fourier transformed infrared spectra (FT-IR) was employed to analyze the structures of CDs-IL. The result is illustrated by the example of CDs-NTf2 (Fig. 2 (c).). The combination of the carboxylic ester C=O is demonstrated by the strong peak at around 1715 cm−1. All of these prove that the target product has the existence of C=O structure. Simultaneously, the peaks at 1052, 1178 and 1345 cm−1 for CDs-NTf2 are assigned to the stretching vibration of C–F and the symmetric, asymmetric stretching vibration of the sulfone group, which proves the existence of anion structure and the smooth progress of synthesis reaction [9]. Similarly, the anion structures of CDs-BScB and CDs-PF6 are also demonstrated (Fig. 2(a)–b.). The peak at 758 cm−1 is from ν(B–O) in tetrahedral boron complexes of CDs-BScB. And P–F peak 835 cm−1 proves the occurrence of anion of PF6 −.

The NMR technique was empolyed to further characterize the obtained CD-ILs. The 13C NMR of citric acid, [C3BHDABCOC3][PF6]2 and CDs-PF6 are shown in Figs. S13–15. Fig. S13a shows the 13C NMR spectra of citric acid (a) and [C3BHDABCOC3] [PF6]2 (b). The resonance signals (Fig. S13a) at 172.08 ppm is due to carbons of the two terminal carboxylic acid groups (c), while one signal located at the lower field region of 175.44 ppm is assigned to the carbon in the central carboxylic acid group (d). The two resonance signals at 72.77 and 42.44 ppm are due to carbons of the (b) and (a) in citric acid. In Fig. S13b, the four resonance signals at 63.08, 57.54, 51.04 and 24.80 ppm are due to carbons of the (e), (h), (g) and (f) in [C3BHDABCOC3] [PF6]2. Combined with Figs. S14a and S15a, it is found that no new peaks could be observed in the blend of citric acid and [C3BHDABCOC3] [PF6]2. However, some new aspects should be pointed out in Figs. S14b and S15b. Importantly, some changes of peaks assigned to carbon centers of CDs-PF6 are observed. For instance, the signal (e) of [C3BHDABCOC3] [PF6]2 that linked to the twin hydroxy has been shifted absolutely from 63.04 ppm to 61.84 ppm (e1), whereas the signals (h, g, f) of [C3BHDABCOC3] [PF6]2 show no obvious chemical shift, while the signals (a, b, c, d) of citric acid became complicated and multiple new peaks are observed in the range of 0–50 ppm and 110–180 ppm. The NMR results indicate that the esterification between the twin hydroxy linked to the carbons (e) and citric acid have completely proceeded, and then in situ carbonization to produce of CDs-IL with IL inserted into carbon sketelon. In addition, the majority of the ILs structures are able to be maintained in CDs-IL.

The chemical structure and composition of CDs-IL were further analyzed by XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. S16, CDs-BScB is composed of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and boron. The fine C1s spectrum of CDs-BScB can be fitted into three peak components with binding energies at 288.9 eV, 286.6 eV and 284.8 eV, which are assigned to COOH, C–N/C–O and C–C/C=C. The peak of N1s located at 402.8 eV belongs to quaternary N in the cationic structure of ionic liquid [50]. And 532.9 eV, 531.9 eV of O1s signals are attributed to C–O and C=O [51]. In B1s spectrum, the peak at 192.4 eV is ascribed to boron atoms of [BScB]- [52,53].

For CDs-PF6 (Fig. 3 ), the binding energy of P2p appears at 136.1 eV combined with the XPS spectrum of F1s at 685.8 eV, which indicates that [PF6]- exists in CDs-PF6 [54]. The signal of N1s located at 402.6eV is assigned to the quaternary N in the cationic structure, and 533.7eV, 532.4eV of O1s signals are attributed to two kinds of O atoms (C–O and C=O). The 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV and 289.2 eV peaks in the expanded image of the C1s belong to C–C/C=C, C–N/C–O and COOH [55].

For CDs-NTf2 (Fig. S17), the peak of N1s located at 402.8eV is assigned to the quaternary N in the cationic structure, and 399.7eV of N1s signal is attributed to [NTf2]-. As for sulfur, the binding energy of S2p is founded at 169.2 eV, which can be ascribed to sulfur oxy species sulfate [56]. XPS spectrum of C1s exhibits four chemically-shifted peak components at 284.8 eV, 286.5 eV, 289.1 eV and 293.1 eV peaks, which are attributed to C–C/C=C, C–N/C–O, COOH and C–F respectively [57]. In O1s spectrum, sulfur oxy species sulfate corresponds to 532.5 eV [58]. The binding energies of O1s at 533.8 eV and 531.1 eV are attributed to C–O and C=O [50]. The F1s signal is at 689.1eV, which belongs to [NTf2]- [59]. The above results further demonstrate that the IL moiety has been introduced in the carbon nanodots.

In order to better discuss the thermal properties of the CDs-IL, the thermal decomposition properties of ionic liquids were examined by measuring the weight loss as a function of temperature. Fig. S18 show the TGA curves of synthesized DABCO-based dicationic liquids. It was observed that DABCO-based dicationic liquids thermally decomposed by 5% of total mass at 257.5 °C for [C3BHDABCOC3][BScB]2, 282.1 °C for [C3BHDABCOC3][PF6]2, 304.3 °C for [C3BHDABCOC3] [NTf2]2. Therefore, the thermal stability of the ILs is arranged based on their initial decomposition temperatures: [C3BHDABCOC3] [NTf2]2 >[C3BHDABCOC3][PF6]2 >[C3BHDABCOC3][BScB]2. On the other side, the residual mass of DABCO-based dicationic liquids at 550 °C is 31.8 wt%, 72.3 wt%, 3.93 wt% for [C3BHDABCOC3][BScB]2, [C3BHDABCOC3] [PF6]2, [C3BHDABCOC3] [NTf2]2 respectively.

Fig. 4 , Figs. S19–21 and Table S1 show the TGA curves and data of CDs-BScB, CDs-PF6 and CDs-NTf2. The 10% weight loss temperature was used as the initial decomposition temperature for measuring the thermal stability of CDs-IL. It was observed that CDs-IL thermally decomposed by 10% of total mass at 132.3 °C, 157.8 °C, 140.0 °C for CDs-BScB, CDs-NTf2 and CDs-PF6, respectively. The thermal stability of the CDs-IL is arranged: CDs-PF6 >CDs-NTf2 > CDs-BScB. On the other side, the residual mass of CDs-IL at 550 °C is 13.6 wt%, 38.1 wt%, 2.5 wt% for CDs-BScB, CDs-PF6, CDs-NTf2 respectively.

The TGA present for each sample a different thermal behaviour. For CDs-BScB (Fig. S19), TGA present a thermal behaviour with three main events, where the maximum loss rate for each weight loss is 189.2, 291.5, 343.9 °C respectively. The first weight loss is attributed to the degradation of unstable oxygen functional groups on the surface of CDs-IL. With the increase of temperature, the further weight loss occurs, which may result from the [C3BHDABCOC3][BScB]2 that inserted in carbon skeleton and carbonization of carbon cores. For CDs-PF6 (Fig. S20), TGA present a thermal behaviour with two main events, where the maximum loss rate for each weight loss is 231.4 and 301.4 °C. The first weight loss is attributed to the degradation of unstable oxygen functional groups on the surface of CDs-IL. Owing to the excellent thermal property of [C3BHDABCOC3][PF6]2, a slight weight loss is observed on higher temperature. For CDs-NTf2 (Fig. S21), TGA present a thermal behaviour with four main events, where the maximum loss rate for each weight loss is 125.7 and 207.4, 343.3, 378.7 °C. The first two weight losses are mainly derived from the bound water and the volatile compounds as well as the degradation of unstable oxygen functional groups on the surface of CDs-IL. And the last two weight losses may result from the [C3BHDABCOC3] [NTf2]2 that inserted in carbon skeleton and the further carbonization of carbon cores. The results suggest that the structure of CDs-IL is quite different and the tiny introduce of IL moiety inserted in the carbon nanodots produce significant impacts on their thermal behaviors of the CDs-IL.

The UV–vis spectra are recorded on 1 wt% CDs-IL/PEG. In the UV–vis spectra (Fig. 5 (a)), a sharp peak at 238 nm was observed, which were attributed to π−π* transitions of the C=C bonds. Besides the C=C characteristics absorption features, the CDs-BScB exhibited an additional absorbance maxima at 301 nm and was attributed to bis(salicylato)borate anion of ionic liquids [60]. While CDs-PF6 and CDs-NTf2 both showed a strong UV–vis absorption peak at 224 nm, which was attributed to various π−π* (C=C). And the solutions of CDs-IL mixing with PEG are transparent under sunlight. The solution of CDs-BScB is colorless and the solutions of CDs-PF6 and CDs-NTf2 are brownish yellow. Under UV light (365 nm), in contrast, CDs-BScB produced blue light, and CDs-PF6 and CDs-NTf2 emitted green and blue green light.

Furthermore, the above solutions exhibited a long term homogeneous phase without any noticeable precipitation for months, which is benefit for practice applications such as lurbicant additives, fluorescence imaging and sensor application, etc.

The photoluminescence spectra are recorded on 1 wt% CDs-IL/PEG. As shown in PL spectra (Fig. 6 ), the CDs-IL exhibited excitation-dependent photoluminescence emission and the PL emission band shift with the change of excitation wavelength, which may resulted from the distribution of different surface states, the wide size distribution, the formation of carbon core structure and the slow solvent relaxation around CDs [61–65]. CDs-BScB has maximum excitation wavelength (λex) at 340 nm and the emission wavelength (λem) at 405 nm. The CDs-PF6 and CDs-NTf2 also have the characteristics of excitation-dependent photoluminescence emission, with maximum wavelength of λex and λem at 440 nm, 518 nm for CDs-PF6 and 380 nm, 458 nm for CDs-NTf2, respectively. The results indicate that three CDs-IL show different absorption and PL emission spectra.

In order to clarify the effect of anionic moiety of CDs-IL as additives on the viscosity of PEG base fluid, the kinematic viscosity of PEG and PEG with 1 wt% CDs-IL were measured as a function of temperature between 40 °C and 100 °C. The results are shown in Fig. 7 . Taking CDs-PF6 as an example, it can be seen that the viscosity of PEG with CDs-PF6 decreases sharply with the increase of temperature from 23.32 mm2 s−1 to 4.14 mm2 s−1, which shows that the fluidity of PEG with CDs-PF6 increases significantly with the increasing temperature due to the kinetic energy of the base fluid increases. The total results are shown in Table S2. Visibly, compared with pure PEG, the kinematic viscosities of the experimental oil samples containing 1 wt% CDs-IL have no significant effect at 40–100 °C.

It is widely approved that the nanoparticles with polar groups can interact with frictional interface thus generating physical-chemical absorbed films that play an important role in avoiding undesirable friction and wear. The tribological behavior of the pure PEG and PEG+1 wt% CDs-IL was measured by four-ball tests at loads of 392 N (Fig. 8 ). For neat PEG, the friction coefficients started from ∼0.08 and gradually increased to ∼0.120 at ∼1700 s, indicating the tribo-films destroyed and scuffing occurred. Unlike pure PEG, in the case of the CDs-PF6 was added, the friction coefficients of PEG reached to minimum quickly (less than 500s) and kept continuously stable during the test under the severe friction condition, indicating that scuffing was inhibited throughout the entire testing process. CDs-NTf2 and CDs-BScB showed similar frictional behaviors with CDs-PF6. The average coefficients of friction of total frictional process of PEG with 1 wt% CDs-BScB, CDs-PF6, CDs-NTf2 are 0.031, 0.030, 0.039, which means 71.8%, 72.7%, 64.6% improvements than PEG (0.110).
With respect to the anti-wear properties, the average wear volumes of PEG with 1 wt% content of CDs-IL are significantly decreased than that of PEG.

where d is wear scar diameter (WSD) and r is the radius of the steel ball.

The wear volumes of PEG with 1 wt% CDs-BScB, CDs-PF6, CDs-NTf2 are 1.092 × 10−3mm3, 0.199 × 10−3mm3, 2.769 × 10−3mm3, meaning 80.7%, 96.5%, 51.1% reductions than PEG (5.667 × 10−3mm3). These results indicate that CDs-IL as additive exhibits excellent friction-reduction and anti-wear performance, which can achieve the maximum reduction of friction coefficients and wear volumes of PEG in 72.7% and 96.5% respectively. The anti-wear and friction-reducing properties in the following order: CDs-PF6>CDs-NTf2>CDs-BScB.
In addition, the CDs-IL fluids keep transparent without any precipitations after tribo-test, which further demonstrate the excellent dispersion stability of CDs-IL in PEG base fluids(Fig. S22).

With CDs-PF6 as a representative CDs-IL, we further performed the tribological properties as a function of concentration (Fig. 9 ). The results show that CDs-PF6 can significantly improve the anti-wear and friction-reduction properties of PEG at low concentrations (0.25 wt%). Moreover, the tribological properties of PEG can be improved significantly with the increase of CDs-PF6 concentration. At a concentration of 0.75 wt%, the wear volume of CDs-PF6/PEG was the smallest, and the anti-wear performance of PEG was optimally improved, which was 97.3% lower than pure PEG. As the concentration increased to 1 wt%, the wear volume of CDs-PF6/PEG was almost the same as that of 0.75 wt%, but the friction coefficient was further reduced, which was 72.7% lower than pure PEG. However, compared with 1 wt% CDs-PF6/PEG, the anti-wear and friction-reducing properties of 2 wt% CDs-PF6/PEG were weakened. This may be due to the competitive adsorption of CDs-PF6 and PEG at the friction interface and the fact that CDs-PF6 itself is more likely to agglomerate at high concentrations, which is not conducive to the improvement of anti-wear and friction-reducing properties.

As listed in Table S3, we have made a survey about tribological characteristics of the reported lubricant additives. Compared with the additives derived from other materials, to the best of our knowledge, the as-prepared CDs-IL as nanoadditive display the best friction-reducing performance, which also exhibit superior anti-wear property simultaneouly than most of other materials.

Since the tested CDs-IL fluids are stable dispersions and fluoresce when illuminated with UV light, we tried to analyze the PL spectrum of the PEG-based fluids after tribo-tests. The contrast photoluminescence emission spectra of PEG with 1 wt% CDs-BScB, CDs-PF6 and CDs-NTf2 before friction and after friction were shown in Fig. 10 . Impressively, the tested PEG-based fluids still exhibited PL nature, however, itself PL intensity has a great decrease in comparison with that of fresh PEG-based fluids, which has a reduce of 36.6%, 76.5% and 82.1%, respectively.

In order to better understand the quenching effect on fluorescence intensity of CDs-IL in PEG base fluids, CDs-PF6 as a representative sample was selected and its fluorescence intensity change as a function of time was further measured. As shown in Fig. 11 , the fluorescence intensity as a function of 5 min, 30 min, 1 h and 4 h was presented. Results clearly demonstrated that fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing friction time. It is noted that the fluorescence intensity of 4 h is obvious lower than that of 1 h. It is widely accepted that quench effects on fluorescence intensity results from defect state emission and quantum confinement effect [66,67]. From the results above, we intend to suppose that the CDs-IL may transfer from fluid to frictional interface, which undergoes physical absorption and tribochemical reactions during tribo-tests, thus quenching the fluorescence and contributing the significant tribological properties.

Since the fluorescence of the PEG-based fluids containing CDs-IL change obviously, we chose this system to observe how the CDs-IL promoted the tribological properties. The excellent anti-wear and friction-reducing performance indicates that CDs-IL in this work can significantly improve the tribological properties of PEG, which is rare in previous researches. SEM images, 3D images and depth of wear scars are shown in Fig. 12 , which is able to provide further support for CDs-IL's anti-wear properties. It can been see that the worn surface of steel ball lubricated by 1 wt% CDs-IL/PEG show smaller, shallower and smoother wear scar, while the CDs-PF6 exhibit superior tribological properties than others.

In order to study what kind of transformation occurred during the friction process, we performed SEM, EDS and Raman analysis on the surface of the worn steel ball. As shown in Fig. 13 , the furrows appear in the SEM images lubricated by PEG and PEG with CDs-BScB, CDs-PF6 and CDs-NTf2. Meanwhile, some depositions are observed to be covered on their worn surface in addition to worn surface lubricated by PEG, which are supposed to be tribofilm formed by CDs-IL (Fig. 13). For identifying the elemental compositions of the depositions, EDS mapping spectra were employed. Three CDs-IL have similar SEM-EDS and corresponding mapping spectra for carbon, which may result from the similar lubrication mechanism of the carbon core of CDs-IL. Meanwhile, the CDs-IL displayed distinguishable active elements distributions and tribological properties owing to different nanostructures and components (Fig.S23-26). To take CDs-PF6 as example, Fe and F were detected outside the deposition region in the wear scar, while large amounts of carbon were observed in deposition areas. Interestingly, O, P and C showed intense response in same deposition region, suggesting the CDs-IL has been transferred onto worn surface and formed a protective film with various active elements under the action of high pressure, frictional heat and mechanical energy generated during rubbing. According to EDS mapping spectra, it is concluded that IL inserted in carbon skeleton decomposed during rubbing, on the one side, P–F bonds broke and combined Fe, on the other side, the residue P and O in CDs-IL could absorb easily on the interface thus contributing to the deposition of carbon core, which may work as “rolling effect” and “nanometer self-healing” and form a protective film containing iron fluoride and phosphorus oxide. Impressively, the EDS carbon mapping gives a good match between the high carbon concentration regions and the depositions on the surface in all three EDS mapping spectra, in addition, the carbon depositions have a size of dozens of microns, which is apparent different with the TEM measurements, suggesting the aggregation of the CDs-IL on the rubbing surface.

The results may also indicate the transfer of carbon core from fluid onto surface by friction-induced effect, on the other hand, resulting in the quenching fluorescence intensity. From Table S4, it was shown that the worn surface exhibited increasing atomic % of carbon from 29.43 to 42.60, decreasing atomic % of oxygen from 9.82 to 4.72 after adding 1 wt% CDs-PF6 to PEG. And the nitrogen (0.29 Atomic %), phosphorus (0.40 Atomic %) and fluorine (1.99 Atomic %) elements are found on the worn surface lubricated with 1 wt% CDs- PF6/PEG, while no corresponding elements exists on the worn surface lubricated with PEG. Therefore, it is highly suggested that the carbon cores in CDs-IL are attached to the worn surface and constitute an effective tribofilm which is beneficial for avoiding the undesirable friction and wear efficiently.

Raman spectroscopy was used to analyze the wear spots to determine the carbon transformation on the interface and understand the mechanism behind the friction and wear reduction of the CDs-IL lubricant. The only important signature in the Raman spectrum of the worn surface lubricated with PEG was iron oxide below ∼700 cm−1, which declares oxidation process happening on the worn surface. For CDs-IL lubricated surfaces, the carbon characteristic bands D-band (∼1310 cm−1) and G-band (∼1600 cm−1) were detected, suggesting the existence of deposited carbon on the worn surface. Their value of ID/IG were calculated as 2.38, 2.29, 2.35 respectively, indicating the existence of a similar high defect level for the deposited carbon. In addition, the peaks of iron oxide were comparatively low lubricated with CDs-IL, revealing that CDs-IL can reduce the oxidation process on the surface of steel balls during the friction, which is consistence with the oxygen content results based on the EDS above.

To further investigate the distribution of deposited carbon on the worn surface, a Raman mapping of the wear surface lubricated with 1 wt% CDs-PF6 was obtained as an example (Fig. 14 ). The change in color in Fig. 14(b) represents the size of the D- and G-band areas and different defects of deposited carbon on the surface. The D-band (∼1310 cm−1) and G-band (∼1600 cm−1) (Fig. 14(c)) detected in the red region of the Raman spectrum were found to indicate significant carbon deposition. However, the black areas have no carbon feature bands. The results show that the carbon has uneven deposition on the worn surface after the addition of CDs-IL, which is consistent with the SEM and EDS results. In addition, iron oxide peak at ∼700 cm−1 was also detected in the black region in Fig. 14(c), indicating that CDs-IL can reduce the oxidation of the steel ball surface during the rubbing process. In summary, carbon from CDs-IL can be deposited on the contact surface to reduce direct contact between the friction pair, helping to achieve anti-wear and friction-reducing effects. On the other side, the defect level of deposited carbon is obviously inhomogeneous, which further indicates the transformation of the carbon occurred induced by friction simultaneously.

For obtaining more information about worn surface and tribo-chemical reactions of CDs-IL after friction test, it is necessary to analyze the pivotal elements on worn surface by using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Fig. 15 , Fig.S27-28). We further analyzed the elements Fe, C, N, O, P and F on the worn surface lubricated by PEG with 1 wt% CDs-PF6. The signal of Fe2p located at 712.2 and 725.7 eV combined with the XPS spectrum of O1s at 531.3 eV, which are attributed to FePO4 and iron oxide [68]. The binding energy of P2p appears at 133.5 eV combined with the XPS spectrum of O1s at 531.3 eV, proving the existence of [PO4]-, which is consistent with the elemental map analysis [69]. The 402.3 eV and 399.9 eV peaks in the expanded image of the N1s belong to quaternary N and organic amine, indicating that a tribochemical reaction occurs during the friction process and new compounds are produced. The fine C1s spectrum can be fitted into four peak components with binding energies 284.8, 286.6, 288.2 and 292.8 eV, which are assigned to C–C/C=C, C–N/C–O, COOH and C–F respectively, suggesting that the CDs-PF6 and its decomposition products form adsorption and tribo-reaction film on the worn surface during the friction process [70]. The peak of F1s located at 684.4 eV is assigned to FeF2 [71]. However, the result cannot be supported by Fe 2p spectroscopy, probably because the signal is too low and/or overlap within the Fe species [72]. In addition, 532.5 eV of O1s signal is attributed to C–O. The similar Fe, C, N, O signals are observed for CDs-BScB and CDs-NTf2. However, it found that a peak of B1s at 191.9 eV is assigned to B2O3 associating to the peak of O1s at 531.9 eV for CDs-BScB, while the N1s signal of [NTf2]- and the F1s signal of [F]- are observed for CDs-NTf2. Based on the above surface analyses, it can be inferred that the CDs-IL adsorb on the surface to form an adsorption film during the rigorous sliding process, resulting in the tribo-chemical products composed of different components mainly derived from anion moiety of IL,which achieve different tribological properties.

Based on the above experimental results, the reasonable lubricating mechanism of CDs-IL based additives is explained. As shown in Fig. 16 , during the rubbing process, the low-energy electrons emit from contact points on the friction metal surface, so negatively charged anions of the CDs-IL can easily combine with positively charged sites of the grooves owing to the friction-induced effect, thus, promoting the aggregation of the CDs-IL on the rubbing surface. Then the lubricating film formed by physical absorption prevents the direct contact of friction surfaces to alleviate wear. Simultaneously, the unique nearly spherical microstructure of as-obtained CDs-IL endows the “rolling effect”, which is beneficial for significantly reduced of friction coefficient. Furthermore, with the prolonging of friction, the deposited physical adsorption film started to decomposed and tribochemical occurred owing to the high pressure, frictional heat and mechanical energy generated by the friction, which not only form a protective transfer film on the nascent metal surfaces but also exhibit a “self-mending effect” by filling the deep scratches, which directly results in the excellent anti-wear and friction-reducing property. Therefore, we believe that IL inserted in carbon skeleton of the carbon nanodots has synergistic lubrication effect with carbon nanodots, thereby greatly improving the tribological performance of PEG base fluid.

In summary, the present work demonstrates the potential of carbon nanodots-ionic liquid (CDs-IL) hybrid nanoparticles in the field of tribology. The CDs-IL was synthesized based on in situ carbonization between carbon precursor and bis-functional dicationic ionic liquid, where the IL could insert in carbon skeleton of the products. For achieving the aim, one kind of novel 1,4-Diazabicyclo[2.2.2]octane-based IL was proposed and successfully synthesized, which owned bis-hydroxyl groups on both side and variable anions of BScB, PF6 and NTf2. The synthesis of the CDs-IL was identified by FTIR, multinuclear (1H, 13C, 11B, 31P and 19F) magnetic resonance, Mass spectra and XPS. Their physicochemical, microstructure and luminescent properties were further investigated by Raman, UV–visible, Fluorescence spectra, SEM, HRTEM and so on. A significant improvement in wear (96.5%) and friction (72.7%) reduction is achieved in case of CDs-IL as nanoadditives for PEG base fluid, which could stand clear either stored for months or experienced severe friction process, proving that the integration of binary components play an important role in enhancement of tribological performance and dispersion stability. Impressively, we found that luminescence quenching along with the frictional duration, demonstrating CDs-IL transferred to interface from fluids and formed carbon aggregates owing to friction-induced effects, thus avoiding the direct contact of frictional pairs.
